metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the lint's behavior when `ctx.state.get('verify_commands')` is empty/absent: either (a) record a coverage-only abstain keyed e.g. `'verify_lint': 'skipped-no-structured-commands'`, or (b) explicitly document that the lint only fires on structured command entries and prose-only verify references are out of scope — and add a fixture/test case for the empty-list path.
findings.finding: The lint mechanism's behavior on an empty or absent ctx.state['verify_commands'] list is unspecified. When no structured commands are set (state has an empty list or the key is absent), there are zero command strings to classify — but the plan gives no guidance on whether the new sub-check should trivially pass, abstain (coverage-recorded), or skip. Given the existing has_verify branch at line 514 already accepts 'test' in plan text as sufficient, the new lint could silently become a no-op for the most common case where commands appear only as prose. This is a genuine edge-case gap in the mechanism specification.
findings.location: What section — 'Language-agnostic + fail-open: a line in an unparseable stack ABSTAINS'; AC2 fixture set
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the What section: 'When ctx.state["verify_commands"] is empty or absent, the lint records {"verify_lint": "skipped-no-structured-commands"} in coverage and emits no lint issues (it cannot inspect prose-only commands).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify concrete matching rules for defect classes (2) and (3): e.g. 'unanchored = grep invocation whose pattern argument lacks a leading ^ or trailing $' and 'unquoted metacharacters = shell word containing unquoted $, *, or ? outside single/double quotes' — or explicitly delegate the decision to the shellcheck prior-art reference.
findings.finding: Defect classes (2) 'unanchored-grep patterns' and (3) 'unquoted shell metacharacters' do not define concrete matching rules — an implementing agent must choose which patterns or characters qualify, making these real design decisions left to the executor rather than specified constraints.
findings.location: What section — defect class list: '(2) unanchored-grep patterns; (3) unquoted shell metacharacters'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Matching rules' sub-bullet under each defect class, e.g.: (2) grep call where the literal pattern string passed does not start with ^ or end with $; (3) shell token that contains any of the chars $*?[] outside of single or double quotes. Alternatively, state 'adopt shellcheck SC2086/SC2046 definitions verbatim' to avoid a bespoke spec.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one 'clean' verify-command fixture to AC2 (e.g., a command with an explicit `test`/comparison/exit-code check, anchored grep, and quoted args) and assert that the lint emits NO finding for it, to guard against false-positive regressions.
findings.finding: AC2's fixture set never requires a *passing* (well-formed) verify command that the lint should NOT flag. A buggy implementation that fires on every line would satisfy all stated ACs: three defect fixtures fire, one unparseable abstains — green, yet the lint is maximally over-aggressive.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC2 to read: '…PLUS one well-formed verify command (e.g., `grep -c "^pattern" file && test $? -eq 0`) that the lint must leave unflagged; unit test asserts no finding is raised for this fixture.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen AC2 to explicitly assert that abstains appear as entries in the single DetResult's coverage dict (not as additional DetResult objects), verifying the aggregation constraint stated in the What section.
findings.finding: The described constraint 'per-line abstains must aggregate into that dict (not per-line events)' specifies a structural requirement on the DetResult coverage dict shape, but no AC explicitly verifies this aggregation behavior — AC2 only checks that abstains are 'coverage-recorded', not that they are aggregated (not emitted as per-line events).
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC2: 'Proof includes assertion that exactly ONE DetResult is returned and its coverage dict contains the abstain entry for the unparseable line (no additional DetResult emitted).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a minimal extensible structure for verify-command lint rules (e.g., a list of checker callables or a data-driven rule table) so WS5/E6 can add defect classes without modifying the p6_ac_quality function body.
findings.finding: The three defect-class rules (pure-inspection, unanchored-grep, unquoted metacharacters) are expected to evolve — WS5/E6 is explicitly scoped to deepen this taxonomy — but the plan specifies no extensibility mechanism (e.g., a registry, plugin point, or config table) for adding defect classes without modifying the core function.
findings.location: What section: 'LLM-tier depth of this taxonomy is WS5/E6 (sibling) -- out of scope here'; AC2
findings.suggested_fix: Represent the three checkers as entries in a `_VERIFY_LINT_RULES: list[LintRule]` (a simple named-tuple or dataclass with `name`, `check_fn`, `abstain_on_parse_error=True`), iterated by p6_ac_quality. WS5 then appends to the list rather than patching the function.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Align the plan title with the body terminology — replace 'blacklist' with 'verify-command lint' (or vice-versa, with justification) so the story title and implementation concept match.
findings.finding: Terminology inconsistency: the plan title uses 'blacklist' but the body and all ACs use 'verify-command lint' and 'defect classes'. An implementer cross-referencing the title with the body could be confused about the intended abstraction.
findings.location: Title ('DET-floor verify-command blacklist') vs. What section and all three ACs
findings.suggested_fix: Rename the story to 'WS4: DET-floor verify-command lint (G-3a)' to match the body and ACs.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan or expand-contract sequence (e.g., warn-only mode flag for the first release) to handle existing verify commands that the new lint rules would flag as defective.
findings.finding: The plan extends p6_ac_quality with new lint rules that add failure modes to an existing check. Previously-passing DET evaluations on real codebases could now fail (e.g., existing verify commands classified as 'pure file-inspection with no assertion'). No rollback path, warn-only mode, or expand-contract sequencing is described.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before committing to bespoke regex, assert in the plan (not just a future code comment) whether `shellcheck` can be invoked on snippet strings (e.g. via stdin or a temp file) and, if so, why it is not adopted — or confirm it is adopted as the implementation strategy.
findings.finding: The plan defers the shellcheck-vs-bespoke-regex architectural decision to a code comment rather than resolving it in the plan itself. Shellcheck is already present in the repo's own shell scripts, but verify-commands are in-memory string snippets (not files), and the plan never asserts whether shellcheck is callable on such snippets (e.g., via a temp file or stdin pipe). This is an unverified capability assertion — the choice of bespoke regex may be forced by shellcheck's invocation model, but that hasn't been confirmed.
findings.location: ## What — 'Consider shellcheck as prior art before a bespoke regex set'; AC3 — 'shellcheck prior-art note recorded in the criterion's code comment (adopt or justify bespoke)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-paragraph 'Prior-art resolution' sub-section under ## What that states: (a) shellcheck is callable via stdin (`echo 'cmd' | shellcheck -s bash -`) and covers defect classes 2 and 3 natively; (b) the decision is either 'adopt shellcheck with a subprocess + fail-open on absence' or 'bespoke regex because shellcheck as a subprocess conflicts with the DET tier's no-external-tool constraint / CI availability'; (c) whichever path is taken, it is decided here rather than at code-comment time.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
